1: Follow the way of love and desire spiritual gifts, especially prophecy: love and gifts are not alternatives but companions. The most excellent way (1 Corinthians 13:1) is the way that properly orients gifts. Prophecy is highlighted because it builds up the community (1 Corinthians 14:3) — the standard of Christ-shaped self-giving love is fulfilled more directly by prophecy than by tongues. The criterion of genuine gift-use is whether it builds up the body of Christ.
2: The tongue-speaker speaks to God, not to people: tongues are a form of Spirit-mediated prayer directed vertically. The utterances are mysteries (mystēria) addressed to God — genuine communication between the believer's spirit and God, but not comprehensible to other members of the body. This is not disparagement of tongues but specification of their scope: they serve the individual's relationship with God, not the community's corporate instruction in Christ.
3: The prophet speaks to people for strengthening, encouragement, and comfort: prophecy serves the body of Christ directly. All three purposes (oikodomē, paraklēsis, paramythia) describe what Christ himself does for his people through the Spirit (John 14:16-17: the Paraclete will strengthen and encourage). Prophecy is an extension of Christ's own ongoing ministry of strengthening his community through Spirit-inspired speech.
4: Tongues build up the self; prophecy builds up the church: the distribution of gifts' scope. Both are genuine gifts but with different primary referents. The self-edification of tongues is real and valid (Paul values it, 1 Corinthians 14:18) but secondary to the community-building that is the body of Christ's primary calling. The love-criterion requires preferring the gift that serves others over the gift that primarily serves self.
5: Paul wishes all spoke in tongues but prefers prophecy: both gifts are good; one is more useful for the body. The prophetic gift is preferred not because it is higher on a spiritual scale but because it more directly serves the community's formation in Christ. The person who prophesies is greater in terms of community-benefit (not personal spiritual status) — a consistently applied love-criterion.
6: What good is unintelligible speech? If Paul came speaking only in tongues, he would not benefit the community without revelation, knowledge, prophecy, or teaching. All four of these communicative modes transmit the content of Christ — his teaching, his acts, his significance, his call. Genuine apostolic ministry must communicate intelligibly because the content (Christ crucified and risen) must be understood, not merely experienced as ecstatic sound.
7: Even lifeless instruments must produce distinct tones to communicate: the flute and harp must play recognizable notes, or their sound is meaningless. The musical analogy establishes the principle that communication requires intelligibility. Spirit-given speech in the community must transmit the recognizable melody of the gospel — the intelligible content of Christ's person and work — not merely produce spiritual sound.
8: The trumpet must sound a clear call for battle preparation: the military analogy sharpens the intelligibility requirement. An unclear trumpet-call before battle is dangerous, not merely unhelpful. Unintelligible speech in the community similarly fails in the moment when the community needs clear direction. The call to rally around Christ must be sounded clearly for the community to respond faithfully.
9: Unless you speak intelligible words, you are speaking into the air: the negative statement of the intelligibility requirement. The Spirit's purpose in giving speech-gifts is that the community might be built up in Christ — this requires comprehension. Speech directed nowhere is wasted; speech that conveys Christ's gospel finds its mark.
10: Many languages in the world, all meaningful: the linguistic observation grounds the analogy. Every human language, however foreign to others, has genuine semantic content — there is no tongue without meaning. The parallel with spiritual gifts: all genuine Spirit-given speech has real content (communication with God), but content without comprehension by hearers does not serve the community.
11: A foreigner to the speaker and the speaker a foreigner to me: mutual incomprehension as the failure-mode of gift-use without love. If tongue-speech creates foreignness within the body of Christ — the community defined by the removal of ethnic and social boundaries (1 Corinthians 12:13) — it is working against the body's Christological constitution.
12: Excel in gifts that build up the church: the governing criterion for gift-seeking. The desire for spiritual gifts must be ordered by the love-criterion — seek those gifts that serve the community's formation in Christ. This is not a hierarchy of gifts in the abstract but a community-situational priority: what the body of Christ needs most is what deserves most emphasis.
13: The tongue-speaker should pray for the gift of interpretation: the solution to the tongues-without-intelligibility problem. If the tongue-speaker can also interpret, the vertical communication with God can become horizontal community-instruction. The gift of interpretation transforms private prayer-speech into communal edification — it serves the body by making the Spirit's communication with God accessible to the whole community.
14: If I pray in a tongue my spirit prays but my mind is unengaged: the genuine phenomenon of Spirit-mediated prayer that bypasses ordinary cognitive processing. Paul does not dismiss this — his spirit is genuinely praying — but he identifies its limitation: the mind's disengagement means others cannot participate. The individual's spiritual experience, however genuine, does not automatically constitute the community's edification.
15: I will pray with the spirit and with the mind; sing with the spirit and with the mind: Paul's integrated worship practice. Both dimensions are necessary: the spirit's direct engagement with God (pneumatic prayer) and the mind's comprehending participation (intelligible prayer). Christ himself worshipped with full integration — his prayers in Gethsemane were both deeply Spirit-moved and fully articulate. Christian worship reflects this wholeness.
16: If you are praising God in the Spirit, how can the uninitiated say Amen? The Amen (so be it, truly) is the community's participatory ratification of the prayer — a liturgical practice inherited from the Jewish synagogue. The Spirit-led worshipper speaking only in tongues excludes the unlearned participant from joining their ratification to the prayer. The community's worship must enable everyone's participation, not just the gifted individual's expression.
17: You are giving thanks well enough but no one else is built up: the distinction between genuine personal piety and communal edification. The tongue-speaker is genuinely giving thanks to God — the act is real and valuable. But genuine individual worship does not automatically create communal edification. The love-criterion requires attending to both: the individual's spirit-prayer and the community's ability to participate.
18: I thank God that I speak in tongues more than all of you: Paul's personal affirmation of the tongues-gift. This establishes that Paul's preference for prophecy in the community setting is not born of personal inexperience with tongues. He has the gift abundantly and values it privately. His argument is about communal context, not personal spirituality — tongues are valuable; they just serve the community most when accompanied by interpretation.
19: In the church I would rather speak five intelligible words to instruct others than ten thousand words in tongues: the stark numerical contrast makes the priority clear. Instruction in Christ requires comprehensible communication. The ten-thousand-word tongue may be genuine Spirit-expression; the five-word instruction may seem modest; but the community's formation in Christ requires the five intelligible words. Quality of service over quantity of spiritual expression.
20: Do not think like children; in regard to evil be infants, but in your thinking be mature (teleioi): the maturity of understanding that the Parousia will bring (1 Corinthians 13:10) must already begin to characterize the community's practical wisdom. The Corinthians' elevation of tongues over prophecy is an immature evaluation — seeing the spectacular gift as superior without understanding the community-building criterion. Mature Christological discernment evaluates gifts by their service to the body, not their impressiveness.
21: A shadow: Paul cites Isaiah 28:11-12 (LXX) — with other tongues and foreign lips God will speak to this people, and even then they will not listen. Isaiah's oracle was a judgment-sign: foreign invaders' unintelligible speech would be the sign of God's judgment on Israel for not hearing his prophetic word. Paul applies it as a shadow fulfilled in the tongues-phenomenon: tongues-without-interpretation in the assembly serves as a judgment-sign to unbelievers (1 Corinthians 14:22), not as the primary mode of community instruction. The OT judgment-sign pattern reverberates in the Corinthian misuse of tongues.
22: Tongues are a sign for unbelievers; prophecy for believers: the asymmetric function of the gifts. Tongues function as a sign to unbelievers — possibly a conviction-sign (the divine breaking in) or a judgment-sign (incomprehension echoing Isa 28). Prophecy serves the community that believes. The sign-function of tongues recalls the OT pattern of signs-for-the-unbelieving (Exod 7-12; Isaiah 7:14) — extraordinary phenomena that testify to divine activity for those who lack faith's inner reception.
23: If the whole church speaks in tongues and unbelievers come in, they will say you are out of your minds: the practical consequence of unregulated tongues. The uninitiated observer cannot distinguish Spirit-inspired speech from pagan frenzy — without interpretation, the community's worship looks like ecstatic disorder. This undermines the mission of the body of Christ in the world: the community that looks mad cannot effectively witness to the rational, coherent Lord who called it into being.
24: But if an unbeliever comes in while everyone is prophesying, they are convicted and called to account: prophecy's evangelistic power. When the community speaks intelligibly in Christ's name — clarifying the human situation before God, naming sin and grace — the unbeliever's conscience is engaged. Conviction (elegchetai) and examination (anakrinetai) are Spirit-mediated encounters with Christ's truth applied to the observer's life.
25: The secrets of the heart laid bare, the unbeliever falling down and worshipping God: prophecy can create the encounter with the living God that results in worship. The falling down (pesōn epi prosōpon) is the OT posture of encounter with the divine (Isaiah 6:5; Ezekiel 1:28). When prophetic speech makes Christ's reality visible to the unbeliever, the result is the same as any genuine theophany — prostration and the confession 'God is really among you.'
26: When you come together, each person has a hymn, instruction, revelation, tongue, or interpretation: the ordered diversity of the community's gathered worship. All contributions serve the single criterion: panta pros oikodomēn ginesthō (let everything be done for building up). The community gathers as the body of Christ, and every contribution should build up that body. The common good (1 Corinthians 12:7) and the building up of the church (1 Corinthians 14:4-5) are the same criterion.
27: If anyone speaks in a tongue, two or at most three, one at a time, with someone to interpret: the regulations for orderly tongues-use. The limits (two or three, in sequence, with interpretation) ensure that tongues serve the community rather than overwhelming it. Christ's body functions as an organized body, not as chaotic noise. The Spirit who gives the gifts also gives the self-control to use them within order.
28: If there is no interpreter, the tongue-speaker should keep quiet and speak to himself and to God: the community's edification takes priority over the individual's gift-expression. The tongue-speaker is not silenced absolutely — private prayer in tongues continues — but the public setting requires intelligibility. The community gathered as Christ's body needs what the body can receive, not what the individual spirit yearns to express.
29: Two or three prophets should speak, and the others should weigh carefully what is said (diakrinetōsan): prophecy is subject to communal discernment. No prophetic word is above evaluation — even genuine Spirit-inspired speech must be tested against the body's discernment. This is the community exercising the mind of Christ (1 Corinthians 2:16): judging all things (1 Corinthians 2:15), testing every spirit (1 John 4:1), holding fast to what is good (1 Thessalonians 5:21).
30: If a revelation comes to someone sitting down, the first speaker should stop: the Spirit's freedom to speak through different members requires the community's flexibility. No individual prophet has exclusive access to revelation during the gathering; the Spirit distributes revelatory capacity as he determines (1 Corinthians 12:11). Yielding to another's revelation is an act of the love that does not insist on its own way (1 Corinthians 13:5).
31: All can prophesy in turn so that everyone may be instructed and encouraged: the universal potential of Spirit-mediated prophetic speech. Prophecy is not the exclusive property of designated leaders but the Spirit's gift given as he wills across the community. The goal — instruction and encouragement of everyone — is the body-building purpose that governs the entire chapter.
32: The spirits of prophets are subject to the control of prophets: Spirit-given capacities do not override the individual's will or rationality. The Spirit who gives the gift does not compel uncontrolled expression; the prophet retains agency. This directly contradicts the pagan model of possession (where the god overwhelmed the person); in the Spirit's economy, the gift-recipient remains a responsible agent. The Spirit of Christ creates order and self-control (Galatians 5:22-23: the fruit of the Spirit includes self-control).
33: God is not a God of disorder but of peace: the Christological grounding of worship order. God's own character — not social convenience or Roman institutional norms — is the reason for orderly worship. Peace (eirēnē) is one of the Spirit's fruits (Galatians 5:22) and one of Christ's primary gifts to the community (John 14:27; 1 Corinthians 20:19-21). Worship that reflects God's character will be marked by the same peace that Christ gives.
34: Women should remain silent in the churches: this instruction — understood in its immediate context as a specific regulation about weighing prophetic speech (1 Corinthians 14:29-33) rather than a universal prohibition on women speaking — grounds the community's practice in the order (taxis) appropriate to the assembly of God's people. The interpretation of this verse must be read with 1 Corinthians 11:5 (women praying and prophesying) and Galatians 3:28 (neither male nor female in Christ). Whatever the specific situation Paul addresses, the principle remains: the gathering of Christ's body requires order that serves the community's formation.
35: If they want to inquire about something, let them ask their own husbands at home: the specific pastoral instruction governing the disruption of worship by questions during the weighing of prophecy. The instruction is situationally specific (wives and husbands in the assembly) and practically oriented — the gathered community cannot function as a tutorial session during the prophetic evaluation process. Domestic inquiry maintains the assembly's Christological purpose.
36: Did the word of God originate with you? Or are you the only people it has reached? Paul's rhetorical challenge to Corinthian exceptionalism. The community in Corinth is part of the wider body of Christ whose practice in all the congregations (1 Corinthians 14:33) should be consistent. No single community has the authority to override the universal apostolic teaching about worship order, since the word that constitutes the community came to them, not from them.
37: If anyone thinks they are a prophet or spiritually gifted, let them acknowledge that what I am writing is the Lord's commandment (Kyriou entolē): Paul explicitly grounds his worship instructions in Christ's own authority. The regulations about tongues, prophecy, and order are not merely Pauline preference but the Lord's commandment — carrying the authority of the one who is Lord over his own body and its gatherings. Prophets who reject Paul's instructions thereby reject the Lord whose word Paul transmits.
38: If anyone ignores this, they will be ignored: the eschatological consequence of rejecting the Lord's commandment about worship. To ignore the instruction about how Christ's body gathers is to ignore Christ himself — and the verb agnoeitō (let them be unknown) may carry the force of being unknown to the Lord at the final reckoning (cf. Matthew 7:23: I never knew you). The stakes of correct worship are ultimately Christological.
39: Eagerly desire to prophesy, and do not forbid speaking in tongues: Paul's positive summary. Both gifts are valid; both are to be sought. The emphasis on prophecy is not a prohibition of tongues but a prioritization within the community setting. The body of Christ is enriched by the full range of Spirit-given speech; the community's life requires all these gifts exercised with love and order.
40: But everything should be done in a fitting and orderly way: the capstone of the entire discussion of gifts. Euschēmonōs kai kata taxin (fittingly and in order) — the community's worship must reflect the God of peace (1 Corinthians 14:33) and the love that keeps all things in good order (1 Corinthians 13:5-6). The body of Christ gathering in worship enacts its identity as Christ's body when its gifts are exercised in the love and order that Christ himself embodies and the Spirit produces.
